claims.claim: We investigate whether labeling evaluation examples by their critical token type (name, number, unit, delimiter) can reveal failure clusters hidden by aggregate accuracy metrics.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Type Importance Labels: Exposing Hidden Failure Clusters in Retrieval and Extraction Systems

claims.claim: Using a template-synthetic dataset of 1,500 examples spanning structured extraction, code navigation, and abstention calibration, we compare four retrieval and extraction systems under both aggregate and token-type-sliced evaluation.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Type Importance Labels: Exposing Hidden Failure Clusters in Retrieval and Extraction Systems

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Aggregate accuracy masks substantial per-type variation: a query-typed retriever achieving 33.60% aggregate accuracy drops to 14.02% on name-critical examples and 24.14% on number-critical examples, while a lexical window retriever at 7.53% aggregate falls to 0.46% on its worst token type.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Type Importance Labels: Exposing Hidden Failure Clusters in Retrieval and Extraction Systems

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A label-aware structural extractor recovers to 100.00% across all slices, yielding +66.4 aggregate percentage points and +86.0 worst-type percentage points of uplift over the query-typed retriever.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Type Importance Labels: Exposing Hidden Failure Clusters in Retrieval and Extraction Systems

claims.claim: These results support the hypothesis that token-type importance labels expose concrete failure clusters and can guide targeted interventions, but the evidence is bounded by the template-synthetic dataset and the structural extractors' exploitation of template regularities.

claims.claim: Standard evaluation of retrieval and extraction systems reports aggregate accuracy, treating all test examples as interchangeable.

evidence_refs.quote: # Token-Type Importance Labels: Exposing Hidden Failure Clusters in Retrieval and Extraction Systems

claims.claim: This practice can obscure systematic failures that concentrate on specific token types—names, numbers, units, or delimiters—that carry disproportionate importance in structured tasks.

claims.claim: A system that appears adequate in aggregate may fail catastrophically on the subset of examples where a single numeric or named entity determines correctness.

claims.claim: We propose token-type importance labels as a lightweight annotation schema: each evaluation example is tagged with the token type whose value is critical to a correct answer.
